candidate_problems.text: What would make higher-level agents genuinely causally real rather than merely explanatory conveniences? The core unresolved issue is to state a non-question-begging criterion for when a system-level description (reasons, goals, deliberation, control) has autonomous causal status, instead of being fully exhausted by microphysical dynamics. Without such a criterion, the conjecture risks relabeling rather than solving the problem.

How can 'freedom requires being caused in the right way' be made precise enough to distinguish agency from sophisticated non-agential control systems? The open problem is to specify which properties—representation, counterfactual sensitivity, error-correction, self-modeling, criticism-responsiveness, knowledge use, etc.—are necessary and sufficient for free agency, and why those properties ground responsibility rather than just complex behavior.

If causation operates at multiple levels, how do top-down or system-level causes relate to causal closure in physics without collapsing into overdetermination or reduction? The unresolved tension is whether agent causation can be integrated with physical law in a coherent metaphysics of levels, where higher-level causes are neither spooky additions nor eliminable summaries. This is the main bridge the conjecture must build to be more than a compatibilist intuition.
